summary: Gruber annotates Apple's white paper arguing against mandatory sideloading on iOS, finding it largely cogent while identifying where Apple undermines its own argument. He argues the strongest anti-sideloading point is that non-technical users would be coerced into installing undesirable apps through work, school, or social engineering — making sideloading not truly a free choice. He acknowledges the Mac comparison elephant in the room, noting iOS is both more secure by design and a bigger target due to its billion-user base. However, he criticizes Apple for conflating App Store trustworthiness goals with revenue goals, citing the Hey rejection as evidence of a conflict of interest. He maintains Apple could defuse regulatory pressure by walking away from maximizing App Store profits. He personally would enable sideloading but believes it would be worse for the platform overall.
key_points: Sideloading is technically the user's choice but socially and psychologically often isn't — work, school, and scams would force non-technical users into installing undesirable software
The fact that typical users install more apps on less capable phones than on more capable PCs is near-proof that Apple's App Store model has been wildly successful
Apple's legitimate trustworthiness argument is significantly undermined by its conflict of interest in using App Store review to maximize revenue, not just ensure safety
iOS is both more secure by design than the Mac and a far more enticing target due to its billion-user base — Apple dances around admitting both are true
Apple should allow apps to direct users to the web for purchases but maintain in-app purchase protections, since the current system provides genuine consumer benefits like easy unsubscription
money_quotes: Technically, yes, on platforms that allow it, sideloading is the user's choice. But socially and psychologically, it often isn't.
As things stand today, you cannot 'mess up' your iOS device by installing the wrong software.
Typical users install more apps on their less capable phones than they do on their far more capable PCs.

I'll admit it: if Mac-style sideloading were added to iOS, I'd enable it, for the same reason I enable installing apps from outside the App Store on my Mac: I trust myself to only install trustworthy software. But it doesn't make me a hypocrite to say that I think it would be worse for the platform as a whole.
The iPhone is the converse: designed first and foremost for the non-savvy user, and tries to accommodate power users as best it can within the limits of that primary directive.
That's a conflict of interest, and it detracts significantly from Apple's entirely legitimate trustworthiness argument defending the App Store model for distribution.
Subscribe to the Times on their website, and you literally have to call them on the telephone and argue with a Times rep whose job is to talk you out of unsubscribing.
No one is going to get tricked or bamboozled into signing up for a $100/year ADC account.
key_insight: Apple's anti-sideloading argument is fundamentally sound on security and user protection grounds, but the company fatally undermines it by using the same App Store gatekeeping to maximize revenue — a conflict of interest that fuels the very regulatory pressure it's trying to fight.
